questions.question: Define proposition and give two examples of propositions and two examples of non-propositions.
keyPoints: Definition: A proposition is a declarative statement that is either true or false but not both
Example of proposition: 'Delhi is the capital of India' (true)
Example of proposition: '7 is a prime number' (true)
Non-proposition: 'What is your name?' (question)
Non-proposition: 'Close the door' (command)

questions.question: Construct the truth table for the compound proposition: (p ∧ q) → r
keyPoints: List all 8 combinations for 3 variables (p,q,r)
Calculate p∧q for each row
Then evaluate implication: (p∧q)→r
Show that only fails when (p∧q)=T and r=F
Final column: T,T,T,T,T,T,F,T

questions.question: Verify whether (p → q) ≡ (¬p ∨ q) using a truth table.
keyPoints: Construct truth table with columns for p, q, p→q, ¬p, ¬p∨q
Show all 4 combinations of p and q
Calculate p→q using implication rule
Calculate ¬p∨q using disjunction rule
Verify both columns match - they are logically equivalent

questions.question: Explain the difference between tautology, contradiction, and contingency with one example each.
keyPoints: Tautology: Always true - example: p ∨ ¬p (law of excluded middle)
Contradiction: Always false - example: p ∧ ¬p (law of non-contradiction)
Contingency: Sometimes true, sometimes false - example: p → q depends on values
Explain that contingency depends on truth values of component propositions

questions.question: State and verify De Morgan's Law for negation of conjunction: ¬(p ∧ q) ≡ ¬p ∨ ¬q
keyPoints: State the law clearly
Construct truth table with columns for p, q, p∧q, ¬(p∧q), ¬p, ¬q, ¬p∨¬q
Fill all 4 rows
Compare ¬(p∧q) and ¬p∨¬q columns
Both columns match - law verified

questions.question: Find the converse, inverse, and contrapositive of: 'If it rains, then the ground gets wet'.
keyPoints: Original: p → q where p='it rains', q='ground gets wet'
Converse: q → p = 'If ground gets wet, then it rains'
Inverse: ¬p → ¬q = 'If it does not rain, then ground does not get wet'
Contrapositive: ¬q → ¬p = 'If ground does not get wet, then it does not rain'
Note: Contrapositive is logically equivalent to original

questions.question: Using truth table method, prove that (p → q) ∧ (q → r) → (p → r) is a tautology.
keyPoints: Construct table with variables p, q, r
Calculate p→q, q→r for each row
Calculate (p→q) ∧ (q→r)
Calculate p→r
Calculate final: (p→q)∧(q→r) → (p→r)
Show final column is all T's - tautology proved

questions.question: Construct a complete truth table for the compound proposition: (p ∨ q) ∧ ¬(p ∧ q). Determine if it is a tautology, contradiction, or contingency. Also identify what logical operation this represents.
keyPoints: Create table with columns: p, q, p∨q, p∧q, ¬(p∧q), (p∨q)∧¬(p∧q)
Fill 4 rows systematically
Show final column values: F, T, T, F
Identify as contingency (neither always T nor always F)
Recognize this is XOR (exclusive or) operation
XOR is true when exactly one operand is true

questions.question: Prove the following logical equivalences using truth tables: (a) p ↔ q ≡ (p → q) ∧ (q → p), (b) p → (q → r) ≡ (p ∧ q) → r
keyPoints: For (a): Create columns for p, q, p↔q, p→q, q→p, (p→q)∧(q→p)
Show both columns match for all 4 rows
For (b): Create columns for p, q, r, p→(q→r), p∧q, (p∧q)→r
Show both columns match for all 8 rows
Conclude both are logical equivalences

questions.question: Write the contrapositive of the following statements and verify that the contrapositive is logically equivalent to the original statement: (i) If x is even, then x² is even (ii) If a triangle has three equal sides, then it is equilateral
keyPoints: For (i): Original: p→q where p='x is even', q='x² is even'
Contrapositive: ¬q → ¬p = 'If x² is odd, then x is odd'
Verify using truth table or logical reasoning
For (ii): Original: p→q where p='triangle has 3 equal sides', q='triangle is equilateral'
Contrapositive: ¬q → ¬p = 'If triangle is not equilateral, then it does not have 3 equal sides'
Explain that contrapositive is always logically equivalent to original

questions.question: A compound proposition is defined as: [p ∧ (q ∨ r)] ↔ [(p ∧ q) ∨ (p ∧ r)]. (a) Construct a complete truth table (b) Is this a tautology? (c) What law does this demonstrate?
keyPoints: (a) Create table with variables p, q, r and all intermediate steps
Need columns: q∨r, p∧(q∨r), p∧q, p∧r, (p∧q)∨(p∧r), final expression
Fill all 8 rows systematically
(b) Final column shows all T values - YES it is a tautology
(c) This demonstrates the Distributive Law: ∧ distributes over ∨
State: p ∧ (q ∨ r) ≡ (p ∧ q) ∨ (p ∧ r)
